coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of fluorescent probe based on molecularly imprinted polymers on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for determination of tobramycin in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Yunyan
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Tobramycin (TOB) plays a considerable role in combating milk spoilage and preventing disease in dairy cows. However, overuse of TOB can lead to nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, neuromuscular blockade, and hypersensitivity reactions. Here, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared with ethylenediamine and citric acid, then molecularly imprinted layers were obtained by imprinting of surface on the N-CDs to prepare Nitrogen-doped carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted polymers (N-CDs@MIPs). The fluorescence emission spectrum of this probe showed a linear enhancement with the TOB concentration in the 1–12 μM. Meanwhile, a detection limit of 99.2 nM was obtained. This probe was not affected by the structural analogs of the TOB and can show high sensitivity and selectivity compared to non-imprinted polymers (N-CDs@NIPs). Therefore, it can be successfully used for the trace analysis of TOB in milk with advantages over other reported techniques such as liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry or various aptamer sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Tobramycin
Fluorescence enhancement

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images and (B) size distribution of N-CDs. (C) XPS survey spectra of N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (D) C1s, (E) N1s, and (F) O1s for N-CDs.
(A) UV–vis spectra of N-CDs/TOB and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/N-CDs@MIPs/N-CDs@NIPs in the presence and absence of TOB.
TEM images of N-CDs@MIPs(A)/N-CDs@NIPs(B); TEM plots of imprinted and non-imprinted layers of N-CDs@MIPs(C)/N-CDs@NIPs(D); SEM figures of N-CDs@MIPs(E)/N-CDs@NIPs(F).
(A) XRD pattern of N-CDs/N-CDs@MIPs/N-CDs@NIPs. (B) FTIR spectra of N-CDs(a)/N-CDs@MIPs(b)/N-CDs@NIPs(c).

TOB concentration of 1–12 μM in the fluorescence spectrum of (A) N-CDs@MIPs probe and (B) N-CDs@NIPs probe. Linear relationship between TOB concentration and fluorescence signal F/F0 of (C) N-CDs@MIPs probe and (D) N-CDs@NIPs probe.

(A) Selectivity study of N-CDs@MIPs probe against TOB and its structural analogs (the concentration is 4 μM). (B) Study of the anti-interference ability of the N-CDs@MIPs probe in the coexistence of TOB and interferes (the concentration of interferes is 20 μM).
The preparation route of N-CDs@MIPs probe.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of fluorescent probe based on molecularly imprinted polymers on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for determination of tobramycin in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tobramycin (TOB) plays a considerable role in combating milk spoilage and preventing disease in dairy cows. However, overuse of TOB can lead to nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, neuromuscular blockade, and hypersensitivity reactions. Here, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared with ethylenediamine and citric acid, then molecularly imprinted layers were obtained by imprinting of surface on the N-CDs to prepare Nitrogen-doped carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted polymers (N-CDs@MIPs). The fluorescence emission spectrum of this probe showed a linear enhancement with the TOB concentration in the 1–12 μM. Meanwhile, a detection limit of 99.2 nM was obtained. This probe was not affected by the structural analogs of the TOB and can show high sensitivity and selectivity compared to non-imprinted polymers (N-CDs@NIPs). Therefore, it can be successfully used for the trace analysis of TOB in milk with advantages over other reported techniques such as liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry or various aptamer sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tobramycin (TOB) is one of the aminoglycoside antibiotics (AGs), which are mainly employed for the treatment of those infections arising from both gram-negative and less often gram-positive bacteria (Glinka et al., 2020). AGs are commonly used to prevent milk spoilage or added to cow feed to prevent disease. Antibiotics cannot be completely removed from milk after conventional pasteurization (Poshteh Shirani et al., 2019). At the same time, long-term residues of these antibiotics in the human body at low doses may lead to transfer of bacterial resistance (Cañadas et al., 2022). In the European Union regulations in 2010, the residues of antibiotics in milk should not exceed 200 μg/kg (Cui et al., 2022). TOB is characterized by high polarity, polycation, absence of chromophores and fluorescent groups, good hydrophilicity, highly water soluble (Glinka et al., 2020). Thus, TOB becomes one of the most frequently used AGs.

Currently, many TOB detection methods have been reported. Chromatographic analysis (HPLC, LCMS, etc.) has high resolution, selectivity and sensitivity, but the complex handling of the sample matrix leads to non-reproducible and inaccurate results. Immunological methods are characterized by high sensitivity and low LOD values. However, unexpected results often occur due to their extremely high sensitivity. In addition, the determination of TOB usually requires LC for quantitative testing due to low reproducibility, which undoubtedly increases the difficulty of the assay. Moreover, both methods often require the use of relatively expensive instruments and the employment of highly trained personnel during testing. Spectrophotometric methods for direct TOB determination rely on derivatization due to the lack of selectivity. In contrast, fluorescence detection has the advantages of simplicity of operation, low cost, and good sensitivity and selectivity. It has been documented that the rapid development of quantum dot-based colorimetric applications and technologies such as molecular imprinting or various aptamer sensors with high selectivity can meet the testing needs and can be applied in areas such as food control and environmental monitoring (Glinka et al., 2020).

Quantum dot-based molecular imprinted polymers (CDs@MIPs) synthesized by surface imprinting polymerization have both the unique optical properties and high-level sensitivity of carbon dots and the advantages of specific recognition adsorption and high selectivity of molecular imprinting (Kong et al., 2016). Fluorescence quench or photo-luminescent sensing occurs between quantum dots (QDs) and target analyte molecules through energy conversion by the FRET mechanism in chemical or physical interactions (Ansari & Masoum, 2021). However, some samples can lead to the inability of quantum dots to detect analytes, resulting in a lack of specificity of the CDs fluorescence sensor (Shi et al., 2019). This is because the fluorescence emission spectra of this samples overlap with those of the quantum dots (Singhal et al., 2022), or the samples themselves quench the QDs luminescence (Bhogal et al., 2020). In contrast, surface molecular imprinting (MIPs) is a combination of cross-linkers and functional monomers in the existence of template molecules to form uniform recognition sites consistent with the structure and size of the template molecule, improving specificity selection (Chmangui et al., 2019). However, MIPs are deficient in the lack of signal export capability. The quantum dot-based molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs@MIPs) synthesized by surface imprinting polymerization combine the unique features of both, while discarding the disadvantages of both (Dehghani et al., 2021). MIPs based on CDs, GQDs, Si QDs and C3N4 quantum dots have been developed. Khataee et al. (Khataee et al., 2018) prepared a molecularly imprinted layer using highly fluorescent GQDs as carriers, and successfully synthesized MIP-GQDs through self-assembly employing highly fluorescent GQDs as carriers, APTES and TEOS as pre-polymerization reactants together with atropine as imprint model, which can be used for the quantitative analysis of atropine. Pongprom et al. (Pongprom et al., 2022) embedded GQDs and porous carbon into molecularly imprinted polymers (GQDs@PC@MIPs) and prepared the obtained nanoprobe with high sensitivity and specificity for the detection of trace amounts of sulfadimethoxine in milk. The method is sensitive and simple to use. Hassanzadeh et al. (Hassanzadeh et al., 2019) prepared highly fluorescent MIP-g-C3N4 QDs with APTES and TEOS co-polymerization for the selective analysis of amikacin in urine samples and water using g-C3N4 quantum dots as a carrier and amikacin as a template. In addition, Xu et al. (Xu et al., 2022) synthesized water soluble Si QDs with high fluorescence and cleverly used the obtained MIPs with SiO2-coated Si QDs for CT testing of actual water. These studies have demonstrated that the coupling reaction of quantum dots with molecularly imprinted polymers is a reliable means of detecting analytes in trace quantities.

Ethylenediamine, ammonia (25 %), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), methanol, glacial acetic acid, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), anhydrous ethanol, tobramycin were sourced from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Kanamycin (KAN), amikacin (AMK), streptomycin (STR), erythromycin (ERY), chloramphenicol (CHL) and vancomycin (VAN) were supplied from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The Britton-Robinson buffer solution was made by using 100 mL of a three-acid mixture of 0.04 mol/L CH3COOH, H3BO3 and H3PO4 mixed with 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution.

Improvement on the previous preparation method (Zhang & Fan, 2020). As shown in Scheme 1, 2.0 mg of citric acid was added to 40 mL of deionized water and ultrasonically dispersed. After that, spiking 1 mL ethylenediamine into system. Finally, it was placed into a stainless-steel autoclave for 2 h. Setting the temperature to 150 °C. It was cooled and the insoluble material was removed by filtration and dialyzed. Finally, it was stored at 4 °C.

Sol-gel technique was employed for TOB preparing N-CDs@MIPs (Wang et al., 2022). First, in a 100 mL flask, 50 mL of ethanol and water (v/v, 1:2) were added. Then, add 5 mL of sonicated N-CDs and 200 µL of ammonia (25 %) and stir for 15 min. Next, 100 µL APTES and 46.8 mg TOB were introduced and mixed for 20 min. Finally, 500 µL TEOS was dispersed into 1 mL of ethanol to the reaction system, as Scheme 1. It was stirred at 800 rpm/min for 12 h in the dark. At the end of the process, the supernatant was discarded to obtain the initial polymer. An eluent of methanol and acetic acid (v/v, 9:1) was prepared to elute the MIPs. Finally, methanol and acetic acid were washed off the surface with water, and dried under vacuum at 50 °C. The solid product was collected and stored at 4 °C.
The non-molecularly imprinted polymers (N-CDs@NIPs) are synthesized as above, ith the difference that TOB is not added.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with an FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). A TU-1901 ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrphotometer (Beijing, China) can provide ultraviolet absorption spectra. The size and surface appearance of N-CDs, N-CDs@MIPs and N-CDs@NIPs were investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM) model SU2080 (Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscope model Teenai G2 F20 (FEI, USA). The chemical bonding and structure of N-CDs, MIPs and NIPs were explored by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of iS10 type (Nicolet, USA). The optimal pH of the system was measured using a Britten-Robinson buffer solution. The selectivity and interfering properties of MIPs were investigated under optimal detection conditions. Record the changes in fluorescence properties of MIPs when they interact with structural analogs of TOB and TOB, respectively, and when interferers are present. The fluorescence enhancement properties were studied by fixing the concentration of MIPs and gradually increasing the amount of TOB.

A 4 mg/mL solution of N-CDs@MIPs was formulated. 200 µL was taken and 200 µL of B-R cache solution with pH = 7 was added and the solution was fixed to 3 mL. The fluorescence signal of MIPs was observed in the existence of TOB and in the absence of TOB, noted as F and F0, respectively. In which, the incident slit and the exit slit were both set to 5 nm, the excitation wavelength was adjusted to 350 nm, and the fluorescence spectrum of MIPs in 380 nm to 600 nm were recorded. F/F0 was used as the fluorescence analysis signal. Each experiment was repeated three times to carry out.

The fluorescent probe based on N-CDs@MIPs was used for the detection of TOB in milk to assess the practical application capability. The processing actual samples was further modified according to the method already reported method. Three milk samples were purchased from the campus convenience store of Shanxi Normal University, China, with the brands Adopt a Cow, Mengniu and Yili. Protein molecules in milk will be adsorbed on the surface of the probe, making it difficult for the antibiotic to bind to the probe and causing false negative results (Zhang et al., 2022). So, proteins and lipids in milk must be removed by pretreatment. 10 mL of milk was mixed with 20 mL of 10% trichloroacetic acid in a centrifuge tube and sonicated for 30 min to remove proteins (Liang et al., 2022). Finally, the samples were heated in a water bath for 5 min (65◦ C). Then, after centrifugation for 10 min (8000 rpm), the lipids were filtered and removed to obtain a clear solution (Poshteh Shirani et al., 2019). TOB was added to the treated milk samples at final concentrations of 0, 2, 5 and 8 μM. TOB in the treated milk was determined using the previously described method and procedure.

N-CDs were produced by the reaction of citric acid and ethylenediamine in water at high temperature. As a result, a large number of abundant reactive groups are generated. The appearance and particle of N-CDs were studied through TEM characterization ( Fig. 1 A). N-CDs are uniformly globular with good dispersion and the average particle size is approximately 4.25 nm ( Fig. 1B). It is suitable for the morphological characteristics of quantum dots.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to study the types and contents of elements and oxidation states of N-CDs. The Fig. 1C shows the full spectrum of XPS of N-CDs, including three elements, C, N and O, with atomic percentages of 55.03 %, 14.10 % and 30.87 %, respectively. Among them, the C1s XPS spectra showed peaks of CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.3 eV), and CO (287.9 eV) ( Fig. 1D). The XPS spectra of N1s had two peaks at 399.6 eV, 400.7 eV, and 401.2 eV belonging to NC, NH, and NO, respectively ( Fig. 1E). The spectra of O1s were fitted to two peaks corresponding to CO and CO with binding energies of about 531.0 eV and 532.2 eV ( Fig. 1F). These results indicate that the synthesized N-CDs have a large number of functional groups about C, N, and O, which are similar to the FTIR data results ( Fig. 4B-a). It is proved that the N elements were successfully doped to form N-CDs.

Fig. 2 A expresses the absorption and emission spectroscopy of TOB and N-CDs. The absorption peak of N-CDs is a broad absorption band centered at 349 nm responding to the π-π* leap of CC groups and the n-π* leap of CO, NH2 and COOH groups (Ma et al., 2018). In addition, there is the absence of spectral coverage among the UV characteristic of TOB and the emission spectra of N-CDs, and no coverage with the emission spectra of MIPs (Fig. S2 A). This indicates that there is no energy transfer between the both.

The fluorescence signals of N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. S2B, indicating that the signal value was strongest when the excitation wavelength was 350 nm. The fluorescence spectroscopy of MIPs (Fig. S2C) and NIPs (Fig. S2D) at different excitation wavelengths were also observed, and the signal intensity of both were maximum when the excitation was 350 nm. It means that the size of N-CDs was not altered after cross-linking polymerization between N-CDs and monomers, cross-linkers, and templates. So no variation of fluorescent groups in N-CDs@MIPs (Cui et al., 2020). This is verified from Fig. 2A and Fig. S2 A, as the emission and absorption spectroscopy of N-CDs and MIPs did not change. Accordingly, the optimal excitation wavelength required for the experiment is 350 nm.

The appearance and distribution status of MIPs together with NIPs were evaluated by TEM and SEM maps of both of them. From the TEM plots (Fig. 3 A and Fig. 3B), it is clear that the MIPs and NIPs are similar in shape, uniformly dispersed, globular in appearance, and with a particle size of approximately 350 nm (Fig. S1E and Fig. S1F). As can be seen in Fig. 3E, the MIPs are spherical in shape, which is due to the aggregation of small particles that occurred during the preparation of MIPs by sol–gel method(Ensafi et al., 2018), and the surface shows a uniform and rough structure, forming a thicker molecularly imprinted layer (Fig. 3C). This is also the reason for the increased particle size of MIPs compared to N-CDs. Compared to MIPs, NIPs also have a rounded spherical shape (Fig. 3F), but because of the absence of template molecules involved in the synthesis, a molecularly imprinted layer with specific recognition of cavities is not formed on the surface. Therefore, the surface of NIPs is relatively smooth (Fig. 3D). This can also be demonstrated from the Fig. S1A and Fig. S1B). Clearer images are shown in Fig. S1C and Fig. S1D.

XRD was performed to identify the crystalline structure as illustrated in in Fig. 4 A. The diffraction of 2θ = 21.25° peak was associated with a graphite lattice spacing of (002) (Poshteh Shirani et al., 2019), demonstrating that the synthesized N-CDs are amorphous carbon-based materials. Furthermore, XRD tests on N-CDs@MIPs and N-CDs@NIPs revealed that both showed diffraction peaks at 2θ = 22.99° (002). This suggests that both are similar in crystallographic structure.

As illustrated in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4B), the structure of the quantum dots was not changed after the molecularly imprinted polymer was formed by coating the molecularly imprinted layer on the N-CDs surface. The peak at 3407 cm−1 is due to C-OH and –OH/–NH stretching vibrations in the carboxyl group of N-CDs, in the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs(a). 2944 cm−1 peak belongs to the stretching vibrations of CH single bonds. 1572 cm−1 peak is considered as NH bending vibrations. The peak at 1391 cm−1 shows the presence of –COOH. The peak at 1186 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of CN. 1653 cm−1 peak is the stretching vibration of CO/CN. These data suggest the N-CDs are abundantly rich in reactive groups like hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups. From the FTIR spectrograms of MIPs(b) and NIPs (c), it can be found that there is no enormous difference between them, which is explained by their very similar structures. It can be noted from the Fig. 4B that both have the corresponding groups of N-CDs in addition to the new 903 cm−1 peaks appearing in the fingerprint region are the asymmetric stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si and SiOC bonds at 466 cm−1 and the asymmetric stretching vibrations of Si-O at 1072 cm−1. This indicates that TEOS was incorporated successfully and demonstrated a strong cross-linking ability. Moreover, the spectra of MIPs and NIPs were very similar, indicating that the TOB was completely eluted down (Qi et al., 2021), which also further proved the appearance of SiO2 layer in the molecularly imprinted polymer. In other words, the molecularly imprinted polymers were prepared successfully.

The luminescence mechanism of N-CDs is still the focus of intense investigation by scholars. The mechanisms proposed for photoluminescence including quantum confinement effect, molecular state, surface state, conjugation effect, cross-linked enhanced emission and synergistic effect (Emam, 2022; Fang and Zheng, 2021; Li et al., 2022). The amount and kind of surface defect states and structural deficient modes in the surface state clearly influence the luminescent properties and luminous mode (Fang & Zheng, 2021). A study has demonstrated that CDs with certain percentage of surface state emission (Wang et al., 2022) will reduce the fluorescence of CDs through surface core defect state emission sites as well as non-radiative relaxation at the surface, while excited state electrons will be returned to the ground state form when not continuously excited (Wang et al., 2022).

Quite a lot of reactive groups, such as amino and carbonyl groups, are known to be present on the N-CDs@MIPs in the Fig. 4B(b). These groups are partly generated during the synthesis process and partly formed on the N-CDs since APTES and TEOS work together. During this time, important alkylamines are also generated. Alkylamines and carbonyl groups initiate non-radiative recombination, which reduces the efficiency of luminescence of CDs. Therefore, the passivation or a reduction of such groups may restore the fluorescence of the CDs signal.

Fig. 5 show the luminescence spectra of N-CDs@MIPs with or without the template. As can also be seen, the fluorescence signal of MIPs after removing the template is 56 % of that before removing the template. This indicated that TOB was easily and effectively removed from the MIPs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of MIPs (black line) is lower than that of NIPs (blue line), which is mainly caused by two parts: one is that the surface of MIPs loses a part of the luminescence signal of N-CDs by forming a thicker molecular imprint layer by the functional monomer as well as the joint action of APTES as well as TOB and TEOS. Whereas NIPs do not form an imprinted layer due to the absence of polymerization of template molecules with functional monomers, resulting in a stronger fluorescence signal of NIPs than MIPs. The second one is that the specific recognition of alkylamines and carbonyl groups on the exposed quantum dots in the cavity induced the non-radiative recombination of electrons, leading to the decrease of fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile it explains the higher optical signal of N-CDs than MIPs and NIPs.

The luminescent signal of MIPs (red line) is significantly enhanced in the presence of TOB. This is due to the alkylamine in the imprinted cavity of MIPs interacting with TOB to blunt the electron non-radiative recombination ability and the fluorescence intensity is recovered. However, the corresponding NIPs also showed a slight recovery of fluorescence intensity, which was attributed to the non-specific binding of a small amount of TOB to the APTES on the surface of NIPs.
To determine the best detection, the detection system needs to be optimized. The variation of F/F0 fluorescence signal values from 380 nm to 600 nm wavelength were observed.

The first is to evaluate the optimal concentration of N-CDs@MIPs in the system (measured by volume) is shown in Fig. S3A. Configure 4 mg/mL of MIPs solution and take 150–350 µL to react with 4 µM of TOB, and express the extent of fluorescence increase by using F/F0 as the signal value. The highest sensitivity was achieved when 200 µL of 4 mg/mL MIPs was used. When the concentration of MIPs is less than 200 µL, the signal-to-noise ratio decreases, causing a loss of sensitivity. When the amount of MIPs used exceeds 200 µL, self-absorption of MIPs fluorescence occurs (Poshteh Shirani et al., 2019) and the sensitivity also decreases. Therefore, the value of F/F0 reaches its maximum when the volume of MIPs is 200 µL, and the concentration of MIPs is optimal for the system at this time.

Next, the pH was optimized using a B-R buffer solution in the range of 3–12. Solutions containing 200 µL of 4 mg/mL MIPs at different pH were prepared in the presence of 4 µM TOB, and the fluorescence signal was measured at room temperature after 60 s. As shown in Fig. S3B, the greatest fluorescence enhancement after the addition of TOB was observed when the pH of the system was 7 and the volume of MIPs was 200 µL. The difference in pH changed the charge of the polymer binding site of the MIPs and the target molecule (Sistani & Shekarchizadeh, 2021). At lower pH, the hydrogen bonding between the TOB and the functional monomer is weakened due to the protonation of the MIPs imprinted cavity at lower pH (pH less than 5) (Ensafi et al., 2018), making the reaction of the MIPs weaker. When the pH is greater than 7, the silica shell is ionized is ionized and surface defects are generated. Therefore, by increasing the solution pH from 7 to larger values, the fluorescence intensity of the MIPs also decreases (Kazemifard et al., 2020).

Also, the response time with TOB was investigated at a volume of 200 µL 4 mg /mL of MIPs, pH of 7.0 and room temperature. As shown in Fig. S3C, the equilibrium between MIPs and TOB was reached rapidly within 60 s. The time had an effect on the TOB detection and on the interaction of the identified cavities. The longer the time, the longer the interaction time between TOB and MIPs cavities, and the stronger the fluorescence enhancement.

Finally, as Fig. S3D, the role of temperature was explored. Within 15–50 °C, the recognition of TOB process by MIPs hardly interfered, so room temperature was chosen as the optimal temperature for the reaction system.

Fig. S4A displays the stability of N-CDs@MIPs. Since MIPs have large particle size, they are prone to agglomeration and sinking. Therefore, in order to establish a reliable and effective recognition system, researching on the stabilization of MIPs is essential. The fluorescence intensity of MIPs was measured continuously at room temperature and pH = 7.0 at 30 min intervals for 3 h. The fluorescence intensity decreased to less than 3.8 % after 3 h, which demonstrated the good stability of MIPs at 3 h.

Moreover, the impact of ionic strength on the recognition system was also explored in Fig. S4B, the effect on the luminescence performance of MIPs was tested by setting the NaCl concentration to 0–1.0 M. The F/F0 values were almost constant at different ionic strengths, which showed that NaCl did not affect the specific recognition of TOB by MIPs.

To investigate the reusability of the developed probe, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs@MIPs probe was used as an indicator for the use of the probe for 7 cycles. To be brief, the fluorescence signal values of the solution containing 200 µL of 4 mg/mL probe were tested first, followed by the fluorescence signal values of the solution containing 200 µL of 4 mg/mL probe and 4 μM TOB, maintaining pH 7 and room temperature conditions. After the test, the probe was eluted twice with 13 mL of methanol/acetic acid (v/v, 9/1) solution, and the template molecules were removed by centrifugation to obtain the N-CDs@MIPs probe again. The above procedure was repeated seven times to obtain the results as shown in Fig. S4C. Obviously, the fluorescence intensity of the probe decreased slightly with the increase of reuse. This is because a small amount of template molecules remained inside the probe unable to be fully eluted, and a small amount of the probe was lost during elution. Fortunately, these effects could be largely ignored in this experiment. After 7 cycles, the probe still maintained 92.89% of its original fluorescence intensity. These results strongly demonstrate that the molecular fluorescent probe has good reusability and can be reused at least seven times.

At room temperature, different volumes of the same concentration of TOB were added to 200 μL of 4 mg/mL N-CDs@MIPs, pH = 7, and the volume was fixed to 3 mL to make the TOB concentration reach 1–12 μM, separately. After the reaction for 60 s, the fluorescence properties of MIPs were analyzed under the conditions of excitation wavelength of 350 nm and both outgoing and incoming slits of 5 nm. As indicated in Fig. 5A and Fig. 5B, when the TOB concentration gradually changed, the fluorescence value of MIPs enhanced and the F/F0 gradually became larger. However, the fluorescence intensity of NIPs was basically unchanged. This is because during the synthesis of MIPs, a molecularly imprinted layer is formed by the joint action of the functional monomer APTES and the cross-linking agent TEOS, and a concentration-dependent fluorescence enhancement occurs on the molecularly imprinted layer as a result of the elution of the template molecules to form a large number of cavities with specific recognition functions. This is the basis for the high sensitivity of MIPs. When the hole binds again to the template molecule it blunts the interaction between the alkylamine group of APTES and TOB in MIPs, thus reducing non-radiative recombination and leading to a substantial restoration of the luminescence signal in MIPs (Wang et al., 2022). And compared with MIPs, NIPs also showed a regular increment, but the degree of increment was much less strong than MIPs, which was due to the fact that no molecularly imprinted layer was formed during the synthesis of NIPs and there was no imprinted cavity. In order words, the sensitivity of NIPs to changes in TOB concentration is low. Therefore, TOB can only rely on non-specific recognition to interact with APTES in NIPs.

Within 1–12 μM, the F/F0 and TOB concentrations of MIPs and NIPs showed good linear relationships in Fig. 5C and Fig. 5D. F/F0 = 1.0317 + 0.0674[TOB] (R2 = 0.994) and F/F0 = 1.0174 + 0.00301[TOB] (R2 = 0.996), respectively, with F0 and F representing the luminescence signal of MIPs in the lack and existence of TOB. The linear equation between the two revealed that different TOB concentrations responded more significantly to MIPs. The MIPs were proved to be highly accurate and reproducible.
The LOD is obtained by the formula(3σ/k) is 99.2 nM.

The fluorescence signals of TOB and the structural analogs of TOB, kanamycin (KAN), amikacin (AMK), streptomycin (STR), erythromycin (ERY), chloramphenicol (CHL), and vancomycin (VAN), were recorded at the same concentrations under optimal conditions, and the selectivity of N-CDs@MIPs and N-CDs@NIPs was investigated. As Fig. 6
A shows, the selectivity of the MIPs is much higher than that of the NIPs when interacting with the TOB. This is owing to the interaction of the TOB with the recognition cavities in the MIPs, while there are no imprinted cavities in the NIPs. Moreover, TOB acted significantly more effectively with MIPs than their analogues. In terms of other words, there are no recognition sites in the MIPs that are fully complementary to the size, shape and functional groups of the six interferes and therefore do not recognize these analogues. Compared to MIPs, NIPs showed relatively weak fluorescence changes in solutions containing TOB and these analogs. This is because NIPs bind to various analytes by nonspecific physical adsorption and cannot cause significant enhancement of fluorescence. These revealed no significant difference in the fluorescence response of this molecular probe in the presence of analogs, which demonstrates its high selectivity. The foregoing results indicate that the blotting technique is an important tool to construct MIPs probe with superior specificity and selectivity (Hu et al., 2021).

The interfering properties of N-CDs@MIPs and N-CDs@NIPs were researched as in Fig. 6 B. The selected interferes were ions and amino acids commonly found in milk and water. Different interferes were added to the solution containing TOB to perform competitive experiments. The resistance of MIPs and NIPs to the interfering substances was evaluated with the addition of 5 times of TOB for each analogue. The fluorescence response of MIPs and NIPs was essentially unchanged, indicating that the interference produced by these interferes was very minimal. This phenomenon may be related to the selectivity of the MIPs and the specificity of the blotting cavity. Thus, the prepared N-CDs@MIPs showed a strong fluorescence response during the recognition process and a high affinity for the template molecule TOB (Hu et al., 2021).

TOB was detected in the treated milk samples and all experiments were performed in three replicates (n = 3). Since the presence of TOB was not detected in all real samples, a spiked recovery test was used for evaluating the applicability of the method in milk. The TOB concentrations in the real samples were detected under optimal experimental conditions and the outcomes are presented with Table S1 . The TOB detections in milk were between 94.00 % and 108.00 % at the three spiked concentrations. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranged from 0.64 % to 3.24 %. This molecular fluorescent probe can be accurately and rapidlyused for the measurement of TOB in real samples.

Table S2 shows the linear range and detection limits of the molecular fluorescent probe for N-CDs@MIPs prepared in this study compared with several reported TOB measurements. It can be noticed that the probe has a better detection limit and a wider range of application.

The present work compares advantageously with the reported methods of Electrochemistry, Fluorescent aptasensor, Colorimetric, Transmission-localized surface plasmon resonance, gold nanoparticle based photometric, Label-free target detection, and LC-MS/MS methods for tobramycin detection. Among them, although gold nanoparticle based photometric and Colorimetric nanoprobe based on dynamic aggregation of SDS-capped silver nanoparticles methods showed superior detection limits, it is undeniable that for methods involving metal nanoparticles, the particles tend to agglomerate and the instability of the experimental system is the biggest trouble.

For the determination of TOB by LC-MS/MS, too many factors need to be controlled, such as obtaining MRM chromatograms, determining the retention order of the compounds, performing flow injection analysis (FIA) on all compounds, optimizing the conditions of the ion source in the mass spectrometer, and selecting the appropriate mode for detection and quantification, which requires very precise details of the experimental operation and takes too long. Fluorescent aptasensor and Label-free target detection for TOB determination are not as fast as this experiment in terms of response time to the test substance (this experiment is 1 min). Electrochemistry and transmission-localized surface plasmon resonance are completely different detection methods from the fluorescent probe developed in this experiment. Although the linear range is relatively wide, the detection limit of this experiment is far better than those two methods.

In summary, quantum dot-based molecularly imprinted polymer analysis was used for the first time to detect tobramycin in milk. TEOS enhanced the cross-linking of the imprinted layers on the core–shell structure of N-CDs and MIPs, forming a more efficient, complete, and easily bound imprinting cavity. The fluorescence signal of N-CDs@MIPs was partially restored when the N-CDs@MIPs used the imprinted cavities to rebind TOB and produced regular concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity changes with increasing TOB concentrations. This molecular fluorescence probe for N-CDs@MIPs has both the unique optical properties and high sensitivity of quantum dots and the advantages of specific recognition adsorption and high selectivity of molecular blotting, and can detect tobramycin in milk within 1 min. The synthetic method and assay have the advantages of low price, high sensitivity and selectivity, good stability, excellent reusability, and easy operation. This study will facilitate further integration of quantum dots with MIPs and apply to rapid and reliable studies of target analytes in several fields.
Yunyan Ren: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Zhefeng Fan: Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135792.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
